preamble.summary: David and the military commanders set apart the sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun for musical service — those who prophesy with lyres, harps, and cymbals. The chapter lists the sons of each music leader: Asaph's four sons, Jeduthun's six sons, and Heman's fourteen sons. The total of those trained and skilled in singing to the LORD is two hundred eighty-eight, divided into twenty-four courses of twelve musicians each. They cast lots for their duties, course by course, the young alongside the old, the teacher alongside the student. The twenty-four courses are then listed in order by lot.
preamble.remarkable: The Chronicler's most striking claim in this chapter is that Temple music is prophecy. The verb naba ('to prophesy') is used three times in verse 1 alone to describe what the musicians do. Asaph prophesies, Jeduthun's sons prophesy, Heman is called 'the king's seer' (v. 5). Music in the Temple is not entertainment or even mere worship — it is a form of divine communication. When the Levitical choir sings, they are functioning as prophets. This theology of music-as-prophecy is unique to Chronicles and profoundly influential: it shapes how both Judaism and Christianity understand the Psalms — not as human compositions addressed to God, but as divinely inspired utterances channeled through musical instruments. Heman's fourteen sons are remarkable — God 'gave Heman fourteen sons and three daughters' (v. 5) as a fulfillment of God's promise 'to exalt his horn.' The number fourteen may echo David's own genealogical significance (Matthew 1:17). The total of 288 trained musicians (24 courses of 12) mirrors the 24 priestly courses, giving music institutional parity with sacrifice.
preamble.friction: Several of Heman's sons' names in verse 4 — Hanani, Eliathah, Giddalti, Romamti-ezer, Joshbekashah, Mallothi, Hothir, Mahazioth — form what appears to be a sentence when read sequentially: something like 'Be gracious to me, O LORD, be gracious to me; you are my God; I magnify and exalt the one who sits in need; he has given abundant visions.' Whether this is coincidence, liturgical composition encoded as names, or a scribal artifact is debated. If intentional, the Chronicler has hidden a prayer inside a genealogical list — a remarkable literary device. The number 288 works out to 24 times 12, but the individual family counts do not always divide evenly into groups of 12, suggesting the system involved some redistribution.
preamble.connections: The musical guilds of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun appear in numerous psalm superscriptions: Asaph in Psalms 50, 73-83; the sons of Korah (Heman's clan) in Psalms 42-49, 84-85, 87-88; and Jeduthun in Psalms 39, 62, 77. This chapter provides the institutional backdrop for the entire Psalter's liturgical organization. The connection between prophecy and music extends back to Saul encountering a band of prophets with lyres, tambourines, and flutes (1 Samuel 10:5-6) and forward to Elisha calling for a musician before prophesying (2 Kings 3:15). The twenty-four musical courses parallel the twenty-four priestly courses of chapter 24, creating a dual rotation of sacrifice and song that defines Temple worship.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּבְדֵּ֣ל דָּוִ֣יד וְשָׂרֵ֣י הַצָּבָ֡א לַעֲבֹדָה֩ לִבְנֵ֨י אָסָ֧ף וְהֵימָ֛ן וִידוּת֖וּן הַנִּבְּאִ֣ים בְּכִנֹּר֑וֹת בִּנְבָלִ֥ים וּבִמְצִלְתָּ֖יִם וַיְהִ֗י מִסְפַּר֙ אַנְשֵׁ֣י מְלָאכָ֔ה לַעֲבֹדָתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Moreover David and the captains of the host separated to the service of the sons of Asaph, and of Heman, and of Jeduthun, who should prophesy with harps, with psalteries, and with cymbals: and the number of the workmen according to their service was:
verses.rendering: David and the commanders of the army set apart for service the sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun — those who prophesied with lyres, harps, and cymbals. The number of those assigned to this work according to their service was as follows:
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb vayyavdel ('he set apart, separated') is the same verb used for Aaron's priestly consecration in 23:13, placing the musicians on the same plane of sacred designation. Most striking is the participle ha-nibbe'im ('those who prophesy') applied to instrumental musicians. The Chronicler does not say they worship, perform, or sing — he says they prophesy (naba) with instruments. This theological claim transforms music from aesthetic accompaniment into a channel of divine revelation. The three instruments — kinnorot ('lyres'), nevalim ('harps'), and metsiltayim ('cymbals') — constitute the standard Levitical orchestra. That David acts together with sarei ha-tsava ('commanders of the army') in organizing musicians is also remarkable: the military leadership has a role in constituting the worship infrastructure.
translator_notes: The verb naba ('to prophesy') in connection with musical instruments appears also in 1 Samuel 10:5-6, where Saul encounters prophets prophesying with instruments. The Chronicler systematizes what was previously ecstatic and spontaneous — Temple music is ordered prophecy, not charismatic outburst.

key_terms.semantic_range: to prophesy, to speak under divine impulse, to make music under inspiration
key_terms.note: nibbe'im (from naba, 'to prophesy') — the Chronicler's defining word for Temple music. The musicians do not merely perform; they channel divine communication through their instruments. This is the theological foundation for understanding the Psalms as inspired Scripture rather than merely human poetry.

verses.text_hebrew: לִבְנֵ֣י אָסָ֗ף זַכּ֧וּר וְיוֹסֵ֛ף וּנְתַנְיָ֥ה וַאֲשַׂרְאֵ֖לָה בְּנֵ֣י אָסָ֑ף עַ֚ל יַד־אָסָ֔ף הַנִּבָּ֖א עַל־יְדֵ֥י הַמֶּֽלֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Of the sons of Asaph; Zaccur, and Joseph, and Nethaniah, and Asarelah, the sons of Asaph under the hands of Asaph, which prophesied according to the order of the king.
verses.rendering: Of the sons of Asaph: Zaccur, Joseph, Nethaniah, and Asarelah — the sons of Asaph under the direction of Asaph, who prophesied under the authority of the king.
translator_notes: Asaph's guild has four sons assigned to lead. The phrase al yad Asaf ('under the hand/direction of Asaph') establishes Asaph as the master of his guild. He prophesies al yedei ha-melekh ('under the authority/direction of the king') — royal authority governs even prophetic music. David directs the worship, and the prophetic musicians operate within that structure.

verses.text_hebrew: לִידוּת֑וּן בְּנֵ֣י יְדוּת֡וּן גְּדַלְיָ֡הוּ וּצְרִ֡י וִ֠ישַׁעְיָ֠הוּ חֲשַׁבְיָ֧הוּ וּמַתִּתְיָ֛הוּ שִׁשָּׁ֖ה עַל־יְדֵ֣י אֲבִיהֶ֗ם יְדוּת֛וּן בַּכִּנּ֕וֹר הַנִּבָּ֕א עַל־הֹד֥וֹת וְהַלֵּ֖ל לַיהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Jeduthun: the sons of Jeduthun; Gedaliah, and Zeri, and Jeshaiah, Hashabiah, and Mattithiah, six, under the hands of their father Jeduthun, who prophesied with a harp, to give thanks and to praise the LORD.
verses.rendering: Of Jeduthun: the sons of Jeduthun — Gedaliah, Zeri, Jeshaiah, Hashabiah, and Mattithiah — six, under the direction of their father Jeduthun, who prophesied with the lyre to give thanks and praise to the LORD.
translator_notes: Jeduthun's guild lists five names but counts six — a sixth name (Shimei) may have dropped from the text, or one of the listed names represents two individuals. Jeduthun is identified as prophesying specifically ba-kinnor ('with the lyre') for the purpose of hodot ve-hallel ('giving thanks and praising'). His specialization is gratitude and praise, connecting him to the psalm superscriptions that bear his name (Psalms 39, 62, 77).

verses.text_hebrew: לְהֵימָ֗ן בְּנֵ֣י הֵימָן֮ בֻּקִּיָּ֣הוּ מַתַּנְיָ֗הוּ עֻ֠זִּיאֵ֠ל שְׁבוּאֵ֨ל וִירִימ֜וֹת חֲנַנְיָ֗ה חֲנָ֤נִי אֱלִיאָ֙תָה֙ גִּדַּ֣לְתִּ֔י רוֹמַ֥מְתִּי עֶ֖זֶר יׇשְׁבְּקָשָׁ֑ה מַלּ֕וֹתִי הוֹתִ֖יר מַחֲזִיאֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Of Heman: the sons of Heman; Bukkiah, Mattaniah, Uzziel, Shebuel, and Jerimoth, Hananiah, Hanani, Eliathah, Giddalti, and Romamtiezer, Joshbekashah, Mallothi, Hothir, and Mahazioth:
verses.rendering: These were of Heman: the sons of Heman — Bukkiah, Mattaniah, and Uzziel, Shebuel, Jerimoth, Hananiah, Hanani, Eliathah, Giddalti, Romamti-ezer, Joshbekashah, Mallothi, Hothir and Mahazioth.
translator_notes: Heman's fourteen sons form the largest musical guild. The later names in the list — from Hanani onward — appear to form a continuous Hebrew prayer when read as a sentence rather than as proper names: roughly 'Be gracious to me, O God, be gracious to me; you are my God; I magnify and exalt the one who sits in need; he has given abundant visions.' Whether these are genuine personal names that coincidentally form a prayer, or a liturgical text embedded in genealogical form, is one of the great puzzles of Chronicles.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־אֵ֨לֶּה בָנִ֜ים לְהֵימָ֗ן חֹזֵ֤ה הַמֶּ֙לֶךְ֙ בְּדִבְרֵ֣י הָאֱלֹהִ֔ים לְהָרִ֖ים קָ֑רֶן וַיִּתֵּ֤ן הָאֱלֹהִים֙ לְהֵימָ֔ן בָּנִ֨ים אַרְבָּעָ֥ה עָשָׂ֛ר וּבָנ֖וֹת שָׁלֽוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: All these were the sons of Heman the king's seer in the words of God, to lift up the horn. And God gave to Heman fourteen sons and three daughters.
verses.rendering: All these were sons of Heman, the king's seer, given through the words of God to exalt his horn. God gave Heman fourteen sons and three daughters.
translator_notes: Heman is titled chozeh ha-melekh ('the king's seer') — a formal prophetic office. The phrase be-divrei ha-Elohim le-harim qaren ('in/through the words of God to exalt his horn') connects his prophetic role to his extraordinary fertility. The 'horn' (qeren) is a symbol of strength and honor. God's gift of fourteen sons and three daughters is presented as divine favor flowing from Heman's prophetic calling — his family's size is itself a sign of blessing.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־אֵ֨לֶּה עַל־יְדֵ֣י אֲבִיהֶ֗ם בַּשִּׁיר֙ בֵּ֣ית יְהוָ֔ה בִּמְצִלְתַּ֛יִם נְבָלִ֥ים וְכִנֹּר֖וֹת לַעֲבֹדַ֣ת בֵּ֣ית הָאֱלֹהִ֑ים עַל־יְדֵ֣י הַמֶּ֔לֶךְ אָסָ֥ף וִידוּת֖וּן וְהֵימָֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: All these were under the hands of their father for song in the house of the LORD, with cymbals, psalteries, and harps, for the service of the house of God, according to the king's order to Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman.
verses.rendering: All these served under the direction of their father in the music of the house of the LORD, with cymbals, harps, and lyres, for the service of the house of God — under the authority of the king: Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman.
translator_notes: The dual authority structure is emphasized: each musician serves al yedei avihem ('under the hand of their father,' the guild leader) and simultaneously al yedei ha-melekh ('under the authority of the king'). The three guild leaders — Asaph, Jeduthun, and Heman — operate as the senior directors under David's overall command. Music in the Temple is both hereditary craft and royal institution.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֤י מִסְפָּרָם֙ עִם־אֲחֵיהֶ֔ם מְלֻמְּדֵי־שִׁ֖יר לַיהוָ֑ה כׇּל־הַ֨מֵּבִ֔ין מָאתַ֖יִם שְׁמוֹנִ֥ים וּשְׁמוֹנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: So the number of them, with their brethren that were instructed in the songs of the LORD, even all that were cunning, was two hundred fourscore and eight.
verses.rendering: Their number, together with their kinsmen trained in singing to the LORD — all who were skilled — was two hundred eighty-eight.
translator_notes: The total of 288 divides exactly into 24 courses of 12 musicians each, mirroring the 24 priestly courses. The phrase melumedei shir la-YHWH ('trained in song to the LORD') indicates formal musical education — these are not casual singers but professionally trained musicians. The parallel term kol ha-mevin ('all who were skilled/discerning') adds that musical competence, not just genealogy, qualifies one for this service.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּפִּ֜ילוּ גוֹרָל֣וֹת מִשְׁמֶ֗רֶת לְעֻמַּת֙ כַּקָּטֹ֣ן כַּגָּד֔וֹל מֵבִ֖ין עִם־תַּלְמִֽיד׃
verses.text_kjv: And they cast lots, ward against ward, as well the small as the great, the teacher with the scholar.
verses.rendering: They cast lots for their duties, one course alongside another — the young with the old, the teacher with the student.
translator_notes: The lot-casting principle is radically egalitarian: ka-qaton ka-gadol ('the small like the great') and mevin im talmid ('the skilled alongside the student'). Seniority, fame, and expertise confer no advantage in the rotation. The master musician and his apprentice are equally subject to the divine lot. This prevents any guild from claiming permanent primacy over the others.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֵּצֵ֞א הַגּוֹרָ֤ל הָרִאשׁוֹן֙ לְאָסָ֣ף לְיוֹסֵ֔ף גְּדַלְיָ֖הוּ הַשֵּׁנִ֑י ה֤וּא וְאֶחָיו֙ וּבָנָ֔יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: Now the first lot came forth for Asaph to Joseph: the second to Gedaliah, who with his brethren and sons were twelve:
verses.rendering: The first lot fell to Joseph of the Asaph guild; the second to Gedaliah — he, his brothers, and his sons, twelve.
translator_notes: Joseph (Asaph's son, v. 2) draws the first lot, giving the Asaph guild the opening position. Each course consists of twelve musicians — the leader plus eleven kinsmen. The consistent count of twelve per course, maintained through all twenty-four lots, creates the total of 288.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשְּׁלִישִׁ֣י זַכּ֔וּר בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The third to Zaccur, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The third to Zaccur — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Zaccur is another of Asaph's sons (v. 2). The formula 'his sons and his brothers, twelve' will repeat for each of the twenty-four courses, establishing the uniform size of each musical unit.

verses.text_hebrew: הָרְבִיעִ֣י לְיִצְרִ֔י בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The fourth to Izri, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The fourth to Izri — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Izri is likely a variant of Zeri (v. 3), one of Jeduthun's sons. The alternation between guilds in the lot sequence — Asaph, Jeduthun, Heman — demonstrates the fairness of the system.

verses.text_hebrew: הַחֲמִישִׁ֣י נְתַנְיָ֔הוּ בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The fifth to Nethaniah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The fifth to Nethaniah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Nethaniah ('given by the LORD') is one of Asaph's sons (v. 2). The Asaph guild now holds the first, third, and fifth positions in the rotation.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשִּׁשִּׁ֣י בֻקִּיָּ֔הוּ בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The sixth to Bukkiah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The sixth to Bukkiah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Bukkiah is the first of Heman's sons listed in verse 4. His sixth-position lot introduces the Heman guild into the rotation.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשְּׁבִיעִ֣י יְשַׁרְאֵ֔לָה בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The seventh to Jesharelah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The seventh to Jesharelah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Jesharelah is likely a variant of Asarelah (v. 2), one of Asaph's sons. Name variations between the roster and the lot list are common in these chapters.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשְּׁמִינִ֣י יְשַׁעְיָ֔הוּ בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The eighth to Jeshaiah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The eighth to Jeshaiah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Jeshaiah ('the LORD saves') is one of Jeduthun's sons (v. 3). The name shares the same root as Isaiah and Joshua/Jesus — yasha ('to save').

verses.text_hebrew: הַתְּשִׁיעִ֣י מַתַּנְיָ֔הוּ בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The ninth to Mattaniah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The ninth to Mattaniah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Mattaniah ('gift of the LORD') is one of Heman's sons (v. 4). This name later becomes significant — Mattaniah was Zedekiah's original name before Nebuchadnezzar renamed him (2 Kings 24:17).

verses.text_hebrew: הָעֲשִׂירִ֣י שִׁמְעִ֔י בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The tenth to Shimei, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The tenth to Shimei — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Shimei may be the sixth son of Jeduthun whose name was missing from the verse 3 list, which counted six but named only five. His appearance here in the lot sequence supports that reading.

verses.text_hebrew: עַשְׁתֵּי־עָשָׂ֣ר עֲזַרְאֵ֔ל בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The eleventh to Azareel, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The eleventh to Azarel — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Azarel ('God has helped') is likely a variant of Uzziel from Heman's sons (v. 4). The name variations between the initial list and the lot results suggest the Chronicler drew from two independent source documents.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשְּׁנֵ֤ים עָשָׂר֙ לַחֲשַׁבְיָ֔ה בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The twelfth to Hashabiah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The twelfth to Hashabiah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Hashabiah ('the LORD has reckoned') is one of Jeduthun's sons (v. 3). This is the midpoint of the twenty-four courses — twelve of twenty-four now assigned.

verses.text_hebrew: לִשְׁלֹשָׁ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ שׁוּבָאֵ֔ל בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The thirteenth to Shubael, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The thirteenth to Shubael — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Shubael is likely a variant of Shebuel from Heman's sons (v. 4). The second half of the rotation now begins.

verses.text_hebrew: לְאַרְבָּעָ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ מַתִּתְיָ֔הוּ בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The fourteenth to Mattithiah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The fourteenth to Mattithiah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Mattithiah ('gift of the LORD') is one of Jeduthun's sons (v. 3). The name is virtually synonymous with Mattaniah (v. 16), both deriving from natan ('to give').

verses.text_hebrew: לַחֲמִשָּׁ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ לִירֵמ֔וֹת בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The fifteenth to Jeremoth, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The fifteenth to Jeremoth — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Jeremoth is one of Heman's sons (v. 4, as Jerimoth). The name means 'heights' or 'elevations.'

verses.text_hebrew: לְשִׁשָּׁ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ לַחֲנַנְיָ֔הוּ בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The sixteenth to Hananiah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The sixteenth to Hananiah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Hananiah ('the LORD has been gracious') is one of Heman's sons (v. 4) and the first name in the sequence that may form the hidden prayer. The name itself means 'grace of God.'

verses.text_hebrew: לְשִׁבְעָ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ לְיׇשְׁבְּקָ֔שָׁה בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The seventeenth to Joshbekashah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The seventeenth to Joshbekashah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Joshbekashah may mean 'sitting in hardship' or 'dwelling in difficulty' — part of the possible embedded prayer from Heman's sons. If the prayer reading is correct, this name-phrase means 'the one sitting in affliction.'

verses.text_hebrew: לִשְׁמוֹנָ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ לַחֲנָ֔נִי בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The eighteenth to Hanani, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The eighteenth to Hanani — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Hanani ('gracious') is one of Heman's sons (v. 4). The short form of Hananiah, it continues the prayer-name sequence.

verses.text_hebrew: לְתִשְׁעָ֤ה עָשָׂר֙ לְמַלּ֔וֹתִי בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The nineteenth to Mallothi, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The nineteenth to Mallothi — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Mallothi ('I have spoken/I am full') is one of Heman's sons (v. 4). If read as part of the embedded prayer, it may mean 'I have uttered' or 'my fullness.'

verses.text_hebrew: לְעֶשְׂרִ֣ים לְאֶֽלִיָּ֔תָה בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The twentieth to Eliathah, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The twentieth to Eliathah — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Eliathah ('God has come' or 'my God has come') is one of Heman's sons (v. 4). The theophoric element indicates divine arrival or presence.

verses.text_hebrew: לְאֶחָ֤ד וְעֶשְׂרִים֙ לְהוֹתִ֔יר בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The one and twentieth to Hothir, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The twenty-first to Hothir — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Hothir ('he has caused to remain/he has left abundance') is one of Heman's sons (v. 4). The name suggests abundance or surplus — fitting for a prayer of divine provision.

verses.text_hebrew: לִשְׁנַ֤יִם וְעֶשְׂרִים֙ לְגִדַּ֔לְתִּי בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The two and twentieth to Giddalti, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The twenty-second to Giddalti — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Giddalti ('I have magnified') is one of Heman's sons (v. 4). Read as a prayer element, it declares 'I have made great' — praising God's magnification.

verses.text_hebrew: לִשְׁלֹשָׁ֤ה וְעֶשְׂרִים֙ לְמַחֲזִיא֔וֹת בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The three and twentieth to Mahazioth, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve:
verses.rendering: The twenty-third to Mahazioth — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Mahazioth ('visions') is the final name from the possible embedded prayer in Heman's sons (v. 4). If the prayer reading is correct, this closing word means 'visions' — the prayer ends with a declaration of divine revelation, fitting for a guild of prophetic musicians.

verses.text_hebrew: לְאַרְבָּעָ֤ה וְעֶשְׂרִים֙ לְרוֹמַ֣מְתִּי עֶ֔זֶר בָּנָ֥יו וְאֶחָ֖יו שְׁנֵ֥ים עָשָֽׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: The four and twentieth to Romamtiezer, he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve.
verses.rendering: The twenty-fourth to Romamti-ezer — his sons and his brothers, twelve.
translator_notes: Romamti-ezer ('I have exalted the help' or 'I have lifted up help') holds the final lot, closing the twenty-four-course rotation. Like the twenty-fourth priestly course (Maaziah, 24:18), this last position carries no stigma — the lot is sacred and the last is equal to the first. The entire Temple musical system is now organized: twenty-four courses of twelve, each led by a descendant of the three great guilds, rotating in perpetuity.
